event: Early humans inhabit the Monte Verde site in southern Chile, one of the oldest known human settlements in the Americas.
The Chinchorro culture of the northern coast begins practicing the world's earliest known artificial mummification of their dead.
Indigenous agricultural and herding communities develop in the northern valleys and Andean highlands.
The Mapuche people establish themselves across south-central Chile, developing a resilient, decentralized society.
The Inca Empire expands southward, conquering northern Chile as far as the Maule River.
Ferdinand Magellan sails through the strait at the continent's southern tip, becoming the first European to reach present-day Chilean waters.
Diego de Almagro leads the first Spanish expedition into Chile from Peru but finds little gold and retreats.
Pedro de Valdivia founds Santiago, establishing permanent Spanish colonization of Chile.
Mapuche forces led by Lautaro defeat and kill Pedro de Valdivia, igniting the prolonged Arauco War.
A Mapuche uprising destroys Spanish settlements south of the Biobio River, halting colonial expansion for centuries.
The Treaty of Quillin recognizes Mapuche autonomy south of the Biobio River.
Chile establishes its first national governing junta on September 18, marking the start of the independence process.
Royalist forces defeat the patriots at the Battle of Rancagua, reimposing Spanish rule during the Reconquista.
Jose de San Martin and Bernardo O'Higgins win the Battle of Chacabuco after crossing the Andes, liberating Santiago.
Chile formally declares independence and secures it with victory at the Battle of Maipu.
A conservative constitution centralizes power and establishes a strong presidency, stabilizing the new republic.
Chile establishes a settlement in the Strait of Magellan, asserting sovereignty over the far south.
Chile enters the War of the Pacific against Bolivia and Peru over nitrate-rich territories.
Victory in the War of the Pacific gives Chile vast northern lands rich in nitrates and copper.
Chile annexes Easter Island (Rapa Nui) in the South Pacific.
A civil war between President Balmaceda and Congress ends with congressional victory and a new parliamentary era.
Troops massacre striking nitrate workers and their families at the Santa Maria School in Iquique.
A new constitution separates church and state and strengthens presidential power amid political turmoil.
Chile becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Poet Gabriela Mistral becomes the first Latin American to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Women gain the right to vote in national elections.
The most powerful earthquake ever recorded, magnitude 9.5, strikes Valdivia and triggers a Pacific-wide tsunami.
Salvador Allende is elected as one of the world's first democratically chosen Marxist presidents.
Poet Pablo Neruda wins the Nobel Prize in Literature.
General Augusto Pinochet leads a military coup that overthrows Allende and installs a dictatorship.
A new constitution drafted under the dictatorship is approved in a controversial plebiscite.
Voters reject extending Pinochet's rule in a national plebiscite, opening the path to democracy.
Patricio Aylwin becomes the first democratically elected president after the dictatorship ends.
The Rettig Commission documents thousands of deaths and disappearances under the military regime.
Pinochet is arrested in London on human rights charges, sparking a landmark international justice case.
Michelle Bachelet is inaugurated as Chile's first female president.
A magnitude 8.8 earthquake and tsunami devastate central Chile, killing hundreds.
Thirty-three miners trapped at the San Jose mine are rescued alive after 69 days in a globally watched operation.
Mass protests over inequality and living costs erupt nationwide, prompting calls to rewrite the constitution.
Gabriel Boric, a former student leader, wins the presidential election to become Chile's youngest president.
Voters reject a proposed progressive constitution in a national referendum.
A second proposed constitution, drafted by a conservative-led council, is also rejected, leaving the 1980 charter in force.
